Article.ArticleTitle: "We don't treat feet, we treat children" analyzing the integration of rehabilitation services into the health system through a qualitative case study of the National Clubfoot Program Uganda.

Abstract.AbstractText: Clubfoot is a congenital birth condition that persists as lifelong disability without early treatment. The National Clubfoot Program Uganda is one of the oldest clubfoot programs in a low-income country and offers a case study for how to integrate rehabilitation services into health systems. The objective of this study was to identify opportunities, challenges, and lessons learned for the integration of rehabilitation services into health systems.
We conducted 61 interviews to learn about the program's implementation and cost structure and conducted a thematic analysis of findings complemented with a bottom-up costing model.

The program's use of existing orthopedic officers to deliver care, leveraging of the public supply chain, and longstanding commitment from the Ministry of Health were found to support health systems integration. Use of a dedicated patient records system, dedicated training and supportive supervision, and filling supplies during stockouts improved quality of care but reduced integration. Overall, the program fills service availability and readiness gaps, but demand side barriers persist.

Findings illustrate how the integration of rehabilitation services into the health system is predicated on the strength of the health system and requires an intersectoral and comprehensive approach to supporting patient identification, treatment, and follow-up.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Department of International Health, Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, Johns Hopkins International Injury Research Unit, Health Systems Program, Baltimore, MD, USA.

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: The feasibility of integrating rehabilitation services into health systems in LMICs is, to some extent, predicated on the strengths and weaknesses of the larger health system, including the ability to identify patients and promote continuity of care.Sustainable financing models and robust referral systems are critical for scaling rehabilitation services.Community engagement, caregiver education, and addressing stigma are critical components in ensuring adherence to rehabilitation treatment; strategies to engage caregivers as active participants in treatment and to lower demand-side barriers to care seeking are critical.A diagonal integration model can serve as a blueprint for integrating other rehabilitation services into low- and middle-income country health systems, though context-specific adaptations and careful attention to broader health systems strengthening efforts are also necessary.
